CURRENT AFFAIRS,

. Dr. Connor Maguire discusses Father Finlay’s Irish
University scheme in this month’s issue of the New Irc-
land Review. Addressing himself chiefly to the finan-
cial question, he writes :—‘Let a meeting be sun;monpd
at Dublin by a Committec of prominent University
men. Invite all who are in favour of fair play to come
and help in the movement. Let a complete scheme

such as that outlined by Father Finlay be put before the

meeting, and the defails settled in a preliminary way.
Then discuss the first and most difficult question of all—
* the money ; how much money is necessary, and whether
we can hope to get it. Father Finlay suggests that
£10,000 be raised annually, and that the buildings
should be of a cheap and temporary nature. In my
humble opinion, it would be far better to attempt to
raise one large sum than to have an annual hat going
round as the chief or sole sustenance of our University.

Everyone knows how tired we get of repeated request
for aid, no matter how deserving the object ; whereas 1
single final appeal will often excite our benevolence, and
its finality will even stimulate and expand our genero:
sity. One mighty effort is ofton easicr than a prolonged
strain.”’

Father Finlay suggested that £10,000 a year collected
annually in the various dioceses, and spent on scholar-
ships to be given to students from the several dioceses
would meet the practical financial difficulties of his
scheme. His scheme is an ad snierim one. 3
Connor Maguire suggests a full-blown scheme providing
an income of £20,000 or £30,000. At 3 per cent.
£1,000,000 would produce £30,000 a_year. Is the Trish
race at home and abroad equal to subscribing this capital
sum? A quarter of a million at 3 per cent. would pro-
duce £7,500 a year, and the members of the Church of
England’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland recently set
about collecting that sum by way of providing: against
a possible shrinkage in the funds for paying their mini-
sters heavy salaries. Certainly Dr. Maguire’s sugges-
tion of making an heroic effort to collect the capital sum
—_whatever that sum may be—is worthy of careful at-
tention. There is no great security in annual collec-
tions. The twenty or so millions of the Irish race at
home and abroad that we so often hear of could raise a
capital of a million by subscribing a shilling a head.
The appeal for a capital sum would—or anyway should
—have a great effect on the wealthy Irish-Americans.
TIrish-Americans stand considerably to gain in many
ways by the moral and intellectual upraising of the
mother race at home. There is besides a psychologicsl
force in a fine wholesale demand that is altogether ab-
sent from a very moderate and more or less begging
request

The Great Southern and Western Railway, as Mr.
Sexton showed at the recent meeting, performed a very
remarkable financial feat. It purchased some business
that brought in a revenue of about £100,000 a year,
and to purchase that £100,000 a year it created a new
preference capital of £4,000,000 at an annual charge
over the heads of the Ordinary Stockholders of from
£160,000 to £170,000. It is not every person that
would be carried away by a flight of financial genius to
raise a sum of £4,000,000 in order to purchase a loss of
£60,000 or £70,000 a year. Even the National Assur-
ance Co., of which Mr. F. C. Pilkington, J.P., D.L., is
a director, might not blush at such a master stroke as
that. Is it any wonder that shareholders represent-
ing £813,283 of Ordinary Stock are anxious for an
inquiry ?

The poll taken at the recent meeting was declared last
Tuesday week; it was too late for comment from us in
our last issue. It will be remembered that the directors
used the proxies to stifle the proposed inquiry into the
business affairs of the Company, and the Chairman at
the meeting announced the proxies before they had been
checked. Sir William badly needs being checked as well
as the proxies.

At the meefing the unauthorised announcement of the
unchecked Sir William was that tho proxies—which ex-
cluded the votes of those who were to vote personally—
amounted to :—For the directors who used them to stop
an inquiry 2,128 proxies representing £2,452,205; for
the inquiry into the business of a Company, one of
whose directors is one of the financial giants of the Na-
tional Assurance Co., 893 proxies representing £779,026.
At the adjourned meeting, and after the proxies, if not
Sir William, had been checked, the Chairman made the
following announcement, which sncluded the votes pex-

- Orange Ulster.

thi

sonally recorded at the first meeting : —TFor tha dn-cctors‘
and baulk inquiry, 2,120 representing £2,439,300; for
the inquiry into this depreciated concern, 8v3 represent-
ing, £790,050. It will be seen that though the latter
figures in favour of darkness and directors include the
votes personally recorded at the meeting, the §Lock re-
prosented is £12,900 less than the unchecked figures of
the unchecked Chairman which ezeluded the votes made

_ There were four scrutineers appointed—two a-side,
One of the baulk-inquiry scrutineers, Mr. Synott, only
attended on one day. The other scrutineer on the side
of the directors and darkness was Mr. Cecil Bailie Gage.
The scrutineers, or, at least, three of them, in the ab-
sence of Mr. Synott, drew up a report. The unchecked
Chairman, however, at the adjourned mecting with
characteristic bad manners and bounce, did not read the
-scrutineers’ report. Now what is the use of a serutiny if
an ill-mannered Chairman refuses to readtheir report?

The demand for light and information, including all

valid proxies received up to 12 o’clock noon on Satur-

day, the day of the meeting, was backed up by £813,283
of stock. Do the direcfors think that they can safely
stand for darkness in face of that demand?

‘We hope Messrs. MVeagh, Flavin, and Vincent Ken-
nedy and others will keep their inquiring eyes on the
Orange part of Ulster, and also on England. But the
Unionist part of Ulster is the more important.  The
thirty odd millions of people in England neither hate
nor love the Irish—they are mostly indifferent, and
know nothing about us, and care less, though their na-
tural racial antipathy to an old racial foe—the Irish—
can be stimulated now and again to violent action. The
chief enemy, and the enemy that we are some match for,
is the bigoted ascendancy that lives in and on this coun-
try. There is a great deal of spurious “ chivalry ’ in
Nationalist Ireland; it will be remembered that Mr.
Field, M.P., was so “ chivalrous ’’ that he signed a peti-
tion for the release of the two Unionist convicts, Ladd
and MacCartney. We notice that Mr. Davitt is pointing
out that Ulster is as much a part of Ireland as Con-
nacht. Quite true; but the Planters are not of the
soil as are the native stock. The Freeman, in an
article, says:— But clearly Mxr. Davitt is right in ad-
vising in his letter, which we elsewhere publish, that the
exposure should be mainly directed upon England. The
Orange representatives of Ulster are traitors to their
own country, eager to traduce and revile her so long as
they find such calumny profitable. But, after all, as
Mr. Davitt reminds us, Ulster is part of Ireland, and
TIrishmen are reluctant to press the case against Ulster.
But it is England that is responsible for Irish Coercion,
and it is all-important to convict the Coercionist as the
criminal.”’  There is a little too much of the quality of
Mr. Field’s “ chivalry ”” for us in that. The Ascendancy
is the immediafe enemy of Ireland, and the enemy that
may be tackled on something like even terms. The As-
cendancy looks upon itself as “ England’s Faithful Gar-
rison ’’ over here. The showing up of Orange and As-
cendancy criminality will have an effect in Treland. Tt
will tend towards cooling the Orange blood, and tend
towards lifting the relative status of the mere Trish who
even yet have none too good a conceit of themselves.
Show up the crime of the “ predominant partner *’ by all
meanis, though it is probable that the British newspapers
will merely not report the showings up; but we submit
that the principal effective work lies in showing up
The Nationalist newspapers will report
these things; and the reports will have an offect.  The
so-called moderate Unionists and the “liberal Protes-
tants ’ stand by whilst attempts are being made to
blacken Nationalist Ireland; they would feel the effect
o,f’a'goqd campaign of denunciation of Orange Ascend-
ancy crime pretty quickly. Ulster industry is thrown
at the head of Nationalist Ireland, and we have heard
much of Ulster “strenuous qualities;”’ let us hear a
little about Unionist criminality.

Local Government Elections usually have no more
@ local intorest. However, the fact that Mr. P.

was unseated by a very small majority in the Swords

Mr. P. J. O’Neill is far more than a local public man ;
he is in the front rank of Ireland’s public men; and
the Dublin County Council over which he has presided
with ability and dignity for such a long period would
indoed be the loser by his absence. We are glad to see
that the Balrothery District Coupcll unanimously
olected Mr. P. J. O’Neill as chairman, and that oﬂ‘if:c

The country generally has little or no interest in local
differences at Swords, but it has an 1}1tcres(: in the fact
that the services of such a distinguished Irishman as
Mr. O’Noill are secured for the premier County C(_Juncﬂ
of Treland. No doubt the Dublin County Council will
unanimously elect Mr. O’'Neill as its chairman; and
all’s well that ends well.

“Treland’s Burden under British Boards,” is the
title of a sixpenny pamphlet, by Mr. C. Lehane, B..L.,
published by Messrs. O’Donoghue and Co. It is a
very interesting pamphlet and deserves to be widely
read. The squandering and blundering Boards that
muddle the affairs of this country have earned all bh,e
light that can be turned on them. M. Lehane’s
pamphlet is full of what we may call glaring statistics
that ought to be on the finger ends of all Trishmen.
The overtaxation of this country and the story of the
squandering Boards are between them enough to tem-
porarily depress even a sanguine Ivish reformer. 'The
worst of the Board cvil is, that like (','hc'Drmk Bill, a
number of people fatten and thrive on it. Treland is
overtaxed, and Boards squander money, but the class
who fatten on the squandering do not mind any more
than Mr. Bung minds the evils wrought by the Drink
Bill of £14,000,000. Mr. Bung would like the people
to drink £28,000,000 a year; and the favoured class
who get such a big slice of the fat jobs in the patronage
of the various Boards would like that twice as much
again should be squandered on useless officials.

This is an abnormal country indeed. It will be re-
membered that the bribe to “ tame’’ Catholics recently
held out by the senile authorities of the Parochial Uni-
versity was promptly knocked on the head by the
Catholic Hierarchy. A distinguished Irishman and
ecclesiastic, Rev. T. A. Finlay, S.J., is at present the
centre of much national interest in view of the self-help
scheme for facing the University problem which he has
submitted to the country. At the time that the
Bishops pronounced against the Parochial University’s
bribe, we remarked that Clongowes might nevertheless
accept it. The Zrish T4mes shook its fist at the bishops,
and of cowrse the nimble Freeman and Independent ran
up the Pastoral Kopje. The Irish T'imes said :— The
resolution of the Hierarchy makes melancholy reading.
They, like an oft-quoted dynasty, seem to have learned
nothing and forgotten nothing; and it may be that
they will complete the parallel by seeing their tem-
poral power pass from them. Their manifesto contains
the same old taunts, the same stale accusations, that
we have read a hundred times.”” And again, the Irish
T'imes said of the bishops’ pronouncement:— Their
words sound like the last despairing battle-cry of a
beaten army. They know that no matter what they
may say or do, the future is not for them. No longer
can medizval terrors be worked with success to stop the
progress of intellectual inquiry.””  Thus spake the
noble Dust Bin to the despised bishops of Ireland last
January ; no doubt the “tame”’ Catholics consider that
that sort of slanging at the bishops as a bench of old
fogies issuing silly fulminations, is “ class’’ and denotes
a “liberal ” mind.

British military band played a selection of music dur-

ing the afternoon,” and we also read that a cricket
match was provided for the amusement of the distin-

guished company. It would not be polite, perhaps,

awn attention to the Wexford Feis, which opens on

‘We scarcely think that the members of the Irish Na-
tional Teachers’ Organisation are to be congratulated on
their President, Mr. J. J. Hazlett. Mr. Starkie wrote
him for information concerning “ the King’’ incident,
and one would think that any man of spirit who had pre-
sided at a formal dinner where certain of the guests and
teachers refused to honour a toast which, as every sen-
sible man knows, is, to a great extent, a party toast, and
a particularly insulting party toast in this country—one
would think that a man of spirit would give Mr. Starkie
a polite and dignified retort that he would not forget
for some time. But this Mr. Hazlett revealed himself,
and revealed his estimation of the teachers, of whose or-
ganisation he is President, in one sentence of his letter.
He wrote : — Shortly afterwards, and when we had just
taken our seats for dinner, about 15 or 20 walked out of
the hall, and did not return. It has been stated that
the party consisted of teachers and guests. This state-
ment is, so far as I can ascertain, inaccurate. I do not
believe there were any teachers among the party, but
apart from all other considerations, I do not think there
is any teacher in all Treland who would pay for a dinner,
and then walk away and leave the dinner behind him.”
So according to the elected President of the Irish Na-
33 tional Teachers’ Organisation there is not a teacher in
Ireland who would, on principle, leave a dinner behind
him that he had paid for. If some Orange teachers
secured that “ To Hell with the Pope ’’ should be given
as a toast the “Idolatrous’’ teachers having paid for
their dinner would not leave it behind them as a protest !

It is rather a new role and not a very enviable one
for Mr. Starkie to write to this Mr. Hazlett with a re-
quest that he should “inform * him about the proceed-
ing at a teachers’ dinner. The docile Mr. Hazlett in-
formed the inquisitive Mr. Starkie, amongst other

. things, that:—" Dinner over, I stood up and proposed
the toast of ‘The King’ in the usual manner, and 1
regret exceedingly to have to report at this stage a
great number of those present remained seated.”” What
a terrible crime, surely, in a country where “ The King"”
has been, to a great extent, collared by a _fachon that
really use it as a synonym for “Down with the Na-
tionalists.”” Mr. Hazlett winds up his letter by say-

i ing:—“I have no doubt whatever but the Executive
of the Irish National Teachers’ Organisation will in the
name of the whole body of teachers entirely dissociate
themselves from and repudiate the regrettable and un-
pardonable conduct of a section of those who attended

- the dinner at Sligo.”” The “ regrettable and unpardon-

able conduct *’ of refusing to honour a toast that in this
country means, for the most part, “ Down with the

Nationalists!”” We wonder what does Edward VIL

think of it all? Now that the Freeman has referred to
the matter, we trust that the arrangement by which
“The King  is toasted at the dinner of the members of

" the British Institute of Journalists over here will be
revised. Surely the Editor of the Freeman and others

of the staff will not be a party to a compact by which

a toast is honoured of which the Freeman said :—“ The

toast of the King’s health has been made a _purty test,
and ‘ God Save the King’' is a party tune in Ireland.

‘They are both employed as an offensive challenge to

~ Nationalists to renounce their Nationality.” We trust
that Messrs. Brayden, M. Bodkin and Donovan will
up this ¢ offensive challenge’’ at the first oppor-

THE LEADER.

A writer in the Unsverse, London, in the course of a
notice of Father Moloney, says:—“ A few days before
the Cathedral festival, a friend from Bermondsey—a
momber of one of the Irish-speaking families Father
Moloney loved—met him and remarked on his exhausted,
delicate appearance, told him that he was killing him-
solf in his work for Ireland, and advised rest. My dear
Dan,” was the answer, ‘this work must go on, and I
raust do it if I have to spend my life on it. Till it is
accomplished I cannof rest.” He accomplished it, and he
is at rest for over, buried among the strangers in the
little Walsingham cemetery in the bleak North of Eng-
land.”” We feel sure that the picture of that cemetery
in the bleak North of England will touch the heart of
every one of our readers who knew Father Moloney. His
death came so suddonly that he was buried almost before
the people heard of it. The picture of that cemetery in
the bleak North of England as the last resting-place of
one who loved Ireland with such a consuming love is very
desolate. THe hoped to live his life in Ireland; he is
dead now, and it is sad, indeed, that Ireland has not
given him a grave. If the London Gaelic League moved
in the matter, thousands of Irishmen would be glad to
do all that was necessary in the way of securing for
Father Moloney a resting-place amongst his own country-
men in Ireland.

NOTORIOUS BIGOTRY IN MEATH R.I.C.

Dear Sir,—Sergeant Anthony Coyle of the Meath
Police completed 26 years service in April last, twelve
of which he passed in the rank of Sergeant and in
charge of police stations.

Joining in 1879, he was selected out of about 100
men by his first District-Inspector to be his clerk, a
position which he held till 1884, when he was appointed
to special duty at a port in the North of Ireland, in
which capacity he acted till 1887, in which year hyme-
neal honours obliged his removal to Royal Meath.
‘When six months in his new station, the District-In-
spector once more recognising his ability, appointed him
his clerk and recommended him for promotion which
he obtained in 1891.

The Inspector-General commended him for good
police duty in 1884 and 1897. The County-Court
Judge complemented him several times for his intelli-
gence in the discharge of his duties. No complaint
was made against him during his 26 years service. A
unique record for a policeman. He was not aggressive.
He was calm and considerate and sympathised with
the people in their afflictions. He loved justice and
truth. Qualities, alas! not calculated to strew a man’s
path with roses in the police. In a country normally
governed, he was gifted with a disposition which would
make him an ideal peace officer.

In 1898 and 1900, he applied for records for good
police service rendered in detecting crime—not manu-
actured crime, and never even received a reply.

_The clouds were now gathering. The bigots were on
lus_ track.  He belongs to the ancient Faith, a “fact
which he could not think it necessary to conceal.

He received the following lottor in last January : —
_“Please inform Sergeant Anthony Coyle that I con-
sider it necessary in public interest that he should now.
retire. If his application for pension be not received
within a fortnight he will ‘be compulsorily retired.

EQUESTED by the editor of the LEADER to give 
my views of the Feis Ceoil week that bas just 
passed by us, I may, perhaps, at the outset recall with 
some amusement how very severely in older days the 
;LEADER hauled up that institution to the bar of public 
Judgment, and what lances it broke with someone-I 
think a member of the F. C. Committee-who came for-
ward. to. cha;mpion the cause of the trembling accused. 
The rnvitat10n extended to me, whose views resemble 
those the LEADER ~hen ~ttacked, appears a proof among 
others that. the ~eis Ceoil. has succee~ed-110t in pleasing 
everyone, 111 cunng all its faults, 111 attaining all its 
possibilities, or, finally, in wholly satisfying the LEADER 
-lmt in getting better understood, and in beiuo- ac: 
cepted, such as. it is, and with hope of improveme~t as 
a beneficent national institution. 
It has been questioned, and that not by more West 
Britons, whether tho Irish name given to the Feis Ceoil 
has been any help to success. 
I should be ,glad to 
IJelieve that it was. 
But it is said to have given rise to 
two sets of misunderstandi~ge. 
The first on the part of 
those (often wealthy, influential, and musically -culti-
vated), who had little or no interest in the general 
Gaelic revival, but much interest in Irish and other 
music. 
Many such persons ,get the ide.a that the Feis 
Ceoil was a function of the Gaelic League, and much 
more a matter of language than of_ music. 
I have 
myself asked people if they were not going to patronise 
an impending Feis Ceoil, and have got for answer : -
" Oh, I don't know any Irish!" 'fhe second set of mis-
m1dcrstaudings arose on the part of some revival enthu-
siasts themselves. 
They thought, appa,rently, that an 
institution with an Irish name could, or should, have 
no other end in view than that which they themselves 
(very rightly) pursued as supremely important. 
They 
would not accept it that the encl of the Feis Ceoil was 
music-music, no doubt, as it should be cultivated in 
Ireland by and for Irishmeu, but still music, and 
nothing else. Hence they expected from the Feis Ceoil 
a more exclusive devotion to the strictly national aspects 
of the art than was the ideal of its founders and sup-
porters. Whatever may be the connection between the 
name and these misunderstandings, one sees with satis-
faction indications that the aims of the Association are, 
at any rate, coming to be better understood, and that 
.criticism-favourable 
and 
unfavourable-is 
more . 
usually aimed from a correct standpoint. 
Except for one important difference, which I shall 
touch on presently, the festival of this year· has con-
tinued the work of previous seasons. 
As to the value of 
that work, when we consider that the income on which 
it is done amounts to barely £1,000 a year, a frequenter 
of its concerts and competitions can hardly deny that it 
has borne fruits which establish a claim to a much more 
generous revenue. 
There is no department of musical 
execution in which it has not striven with success to 
improve the general level. 
The high, artistic, and tech-
nical standard of the test-pieces, the qualifications and 
impartiality of the judges, the bringing of expert musi-
cal opinion into touch with the humblest tyro-these 
have told in favour of every form of musical perform-
ance- of the 'cellest and the piper, of solo and chorus, 
of convent girl and artisan. It is true, and most regret-
table, that these advantages have been very slightly 
participated in outside of our metropolis. 
Only a few 
other towns have taken up work on the Feis Ceoil lines. 
But, as far as I can see, the F . C. cannot be blamed for 
this deficiency. 
There are two explanations-the prizes 
offered are not high enough, the railway fares are not 
low enough, to induce musical aspirants from the country 
to risk the trouble, expense, and loss of time involved 
in aiming at a Feis C~oil victory. 
The railways might 
d-o more than they do ; the Association, with its present 
income, could hardly do more than it does. 
As for the 
idea that the Feis Ceoil could locate itself, year by year, 
in various provincial centres, there is one obstacle which 
some critics and advisers have overlooked-namely, the 
lack of suitable public halls. 
Such a lack (to give a 
notable instance), is sufficient to prevent the Feis being 
held in Cork. 
Undoubtedly this question of getting a 
hold cin the country in general is -a most important one 
for the future of the institution. 
So long as it can 
f~frly be stigmatised by the Corkoniai1 or the Galwegian 
as merely "a Dublin affair," it will not have attained 
the rank of a national festival. 
And what a boon it 
would be if, north and .south, east and west, there were 
developed snch a growth of interest in good music,-and 
of the power of singing it, as has been inaugurated for 
Dublin by the choruses, quartettes, trios, etc., of the 
"commercial" and" trades" sections of t,he F. C. com-
peUtions (I speak only of what the F. C. 
has itself 
created). 
'.rhe development of choral music and part-
singing is a most important element in social progress, 
and, perhaps, oven 11101'0 so for the dull rural town than 
for a capital. 
But for the present it seems impossible 
to move the Feis, even for one year, out of Dublin. 
In its work o-f bringing out unpublished Irish airs, 
and in its attention to the strictly traditional aspects of 
Irish music the Feis Ceoil has (as already hinted) caused 
c011siclerable disappointment.

must be confessed that many difficult problems still
hang around its nature and proper oxecution; so that,
perhaps, it is as well that for the present its practical
care should be largely left to the Gaelic League, and its
theoretical investigation to individual students like
Fat_‘.hcr Bewerunge, Dr. Henebery, and Mr. Rogers.
This year the Feis Ceoil opened its ovening celebrations
with a lecture entitled the “ Evolution of Irish Folk-
Song;’* and unluckily this lecture was not calculated to
heighten the reputation of the Feis Ceoil as a form of
enlightenment on our native music.

As to the competitions in singing and ‘playing of the
recent festival, one had to rogret (as in previous years)
the small number of entries in certain sections where
one .would have liked to see (or hear) a warm contest.
Most of our big commercial houses, and nearly all our
trades, are still “out of it ’’ in choral music. ~Varian,
Pim, Jacob, why do these names (all, I think, Quaker
ones) stand solitary? Why were the little pupils of the
Gardiner St. Sisters of Charity left alone to give us a
delightful rendering of “ The Bells of Shandon’’ and
“ When Winds Breathe Soft?”’ I should be very glad,
too, to see other branches of the Gaelic League imitate
the example of the Brian Boru Branch, and add to their
own special concerns the congenial and wholly profitable
work of high-class part-singing. Then there was disap-
pointment in the matter of bands and orchestras. The
Leinster School Orchestra found no competitors, and
only one Boys’ Brigade Band came forward. Dublin,
one would fancy, ought to have done better, even with
the rest of the country sitting still. For two or three
more of such entries one would readily sacrifice many of
the fifty-four mezzo-soprani and of the forty-one pianists
—though these pianists were really a good lot.

Of course, the giving of larger prizes would quickly
put a stir into departments of Irish musical life, in which
(as revealed by the Feis Ceoil) animation now seems
languid or lacking.” " But the work depends upon the
fluctuations and treacheries of public support, concern-
ing which few Irish musical enterprises have a cheerful
tale to tell; and without more annual sub-
scriptions from the well-to-do, more gate-money at con-
certs and competitions, more competitors’ fees (though
these are a trifle), the work cannot progress—can it even
continue? The present has been a year of economy.
By cutting off composers’ competitions, and the expenses
involved in the production of prize competitions, the
Committee have sought to retrieve their loss of last year.
‘We hope they have fully succeeded in their aim.  The
festival has not, I think, lost greatly in interest through
the subtraction of the prize-competition element. Many
ask whether it is at all necessary or desirable to stimulate
the production of important musical works so often as
once a year, or even once in two years. But though we

_ answer this question in the negative, it is still clear

that the expense of composers’ competitions must fall,
at-intervals more or less frequent, upon the shoulders of
the Feis Committee; and the public must show, in a
practical way; its desire, if it has a desire, to elicit more
such works as Hamilton Harty’s “Irish Symphony,”’
and to develop more orchestras and conductors able to
perform them. So far as we can see, the moneys placed
in the hands of the Committee are utilised with admir-
able economy. I take up at hap-hazard the accounts as
published for 1901, and find that out of an income of
about £1;000 from all sources, £340 were spent on prizes
£167 on adjudicators, £216 on various
strictly musical expenses, while only £91 is allowed for

Scotland, who dredged the oyster-beds regardless of
season or size of the oyster taken, and who trawled the
fishing grounds in a way that killed the young fry and
drove the more mature fish to other spawning grounds.
The hint has been taken. An additional boat has been
put on patrol duty. The steamer belonging to the
Congested Districts’ Board—she has an .Irish name—
the “Granuaile,” and the “ Helga *’ the steam cruiser
of the Department are now on patrol duty. Further,
with the sanction of the Naval authorities, instructions
have been issued to every coastguard station in Ireland
to report by telegram to the Departmentin Merrion
Row, any breaches of the regulations relating to trawl-
ing; whether any foreign hoats come within the three-
mile limit of the shore; and any breach of the fishery
laws they may observe.

These regulations it is to be hoped will ensure a
much better system of check on the depredators who
have so ruined the fishing grounds.  The Department
by previous arrangement, knows exactly where to find
the patrol steamers day by day if they are not within
reach of the Coastguard and Custom Officials, and the
Captains of the respective steamers have been furnished
with warrants which constitute them a naval police, and
empower them to arrest and detain the delinquents,
seize the vessels and fishing gear, bring the offenders
before the nearest Magistrate, and have them sum-
marily dealt with by fine and imprisonment. The Cap-
tains of the patrol boats can further examine the fishing
gear of the boats who have a legitimate right to be
on the fishing grounds, and see if the fishing gear 1s
made according to law; to see if the meshes of the net
are the right size, and the diameter of the rings forming
the oyster-bag trawl are not smaller than the pre-
scribed size. But we may ask, why were not all these
regulations promulgated sooner? On the English and
Scotch fishing grounds these rules have been in force
for the last twenty years; why not have copied them
sooner? To make the patrol duty really effective, how-
ever, at least two additional swift steamers or gun-
hoats should be added to the two already on duty, and
as an incentive, a portion of the fine infiicted ought to
be given to the coastguards and the crews of the
steamers as rewards to make them active and vigilant.

Turning now to a matter which has been before Par-
liament in a desultory way, off and on for the last two
sessions. What is the objection of Sir Horace Plunkett
and his Board to giving to the clerks in the Veterinary
Branch of the Department, the usual stipend which men
having the knowledge and experience they have, in-
variably get. It secoms there has been an all-vound
raising of the salaries and increments in the other
branches of the Department, but, as would appear by
the questions so repeatedly asked in the Ifouse of
Commons, the Veterinary staff, which is to a man almost
Catholic, has been left out in the cold. I can scarcely
believe the fact of their religion would militate against

A REVIEW OFf EDUCATION IN IRELAND—III—
(Continued.)

Down to 1878 Catholic Intermediate Education was
absolutely unendowed. There were endowments of Inter-
| mediate Education—and rich ones—but non-Catholics
{ exclusively enjoyed them. In that year Earl Cairns intro-
| duced the Intermediate Education (Ireland) Bill, and it
{ was without much difficulty passed into law. The sum of
£1,000,000 out of the surplus fund of the Disestablished
Church was set aside for the purpose. All Irish In-
termediate Schools might win their share of the annual
revenue which that capital afforded, according as each
proved its efficiency in open competition.  But the
! Catholic and the non-Catholic Schools entered the arena
i under widely different circumstances. The latter had
their endowments, and a supposed educational supe-
riority. The Catholic Schools had no endowments, and
i a supposed inferiority. The privilege of competing was
simply given to them; that was all. The prestige of
non-Catholic education had become a tradition in Ire-
land. Quite naturally. ' I have been told by some of
those who were teaching in Catholic Schools at the
time, that they sent their pupils to the first year’s exami-
nations with fear and trembling. But the result was a
revelation. Their pupils left their competitors behind ;
and their victory, year by year ever since, has brought
educational prestige over to their side. The Interme-
diate System has had this important effect : it has made
Catholic teachers and pupils conscious of an educational
superiority of which fhey were unconscious before. They
have come to believe in themselves; and non-Catholics,
amongst them Dr. Archdall the Protestant bishop of
Killaloe, and Dr. Bunbury, Protestant hishop of Lime-
rick, in his notorious Synodal Address three years ago,
have openly admitted their superiority. The
system has this other important result : it is in principle
a denominational system. ~. The old subterfuge of refus-

& ing public money to denominational Colleges can be con-
sistently resorted to no more. The principle of secu-
larism has ceased to be sacred. Competitive tests have

& ~ exploded its excellence; the legisla itself has rele-

gated it to the past as a public principle. If I am re-

minded that the Intermediate system is educationally
~ defective, that victory under it does not connote true
_ educational efficiency, I readily assent. Public com-
_petition has its right place when one’s education, such
s it may be, is completed ; but I think that introduced
efore that term it tends to vitiate the process of educa-
on. But here again, Catholics have not created the
stem ;* they have acepted it for want of better. They

have gone into an arena not of their own choice, and
they have come out with the laurels. If, therefore,
Catholic Intermediate Schools be still said to have their
shortcomings, let the blame be not put on then.:l, but on
the system under which they have to work. It is worthy
of remark that the Christian Brothers’ Schools, which
are absolutely Catholic, are of admitted excellence. They
have never come under the influence of Government
officialdom, nor have they been originally designed under
Government patronage, but precisely against it. More-
over, it is doubtful whether since they have partially
changed their primary character and gone under the In-
termediate System, they have maintained their general
excellence as primary schools.

Let us now consider University education. As a
Catholic nation, it is our anomalous fate to be ac-
quainted with non-Catholic Universities only. Non-
Catholic thought permeates our literature, science, and
art. We have been reared in that atmosphere. As in
our fathers’ time, to speak of the gentry was to mean
Protestants ; so the delusion of identifying Protestantism
or secularism with University life came upon us also,
and is living still—a lingering relic of the past. We,
have thus been brought by the force of circumstances to
look on University life and the Catholic idea as almost
incompatible. Little blame to us: we have been denied
the opportunity and the right of witnessing in our
midst the work of a Catholic University untrammelled
by difficulties. It has been the persistent policy of our
neighbours to represent the Catholic Church as the
nurse of obscurantism. They first robbed, and then
reviled her; and a few Catholics help to keep the illu-
sion alive, forgetting that they are defaming their own
mother and cutting their own throats. That was the
condition of things before the Royal University came
in 1879; but it is so no longer. The Royal University
is not in strictness a University at all; it is simply an
examining body. There can be no doubt that its Degrees
require much more positive knowledge than those of Tri-
nity College; nevertheless, many have obtained them by
private study as well as those who have undergone a
formal course of teaching; and anyone who has had ex-
perience in these things knows the difference which that
makes in most cases. Trinity College gives Degrees to
candidatee from private study also.

As under the Intermediate System, so also in the
Royal University, the Catholic Colleges had to enter the
lists in their poverty, whilst the non-Catholic Colleges
had their endowments. The three Queen’s Colleges had
been at work for thirty years.  £100,000 of public
money was originally spent on their buildings and
equipment ; and they enjoyed a revenue of £30,000 a
year. In an able pamphlet published last year, Dr.
Delaney, S.J.,* points out that “ the estimates for the
past three years, 1901-2-3, show that the total expendi-
ture on the three Colleges in these years amounted re-
spectively to £34,098, £34,916, and £34,966; and this
last sum £34,966 is also the estimate for the current
year 1903-4.”” Including the charge involved in the
original outlay, the total cost to the taxpayer is
£38,000 a year. The Catholic College had to
compete against those three Colleges, with their en-
dowments and their prestige, in which the education
given, according to Dr. Johnstone Stoney, “aims at
being the best which the twentieth century is able to
develop.”” + Let us see some of the results. From the
year 1884 to 1896, of the candidates from the Catholic
University College, Dublin, who passed the B.A. Degree
of the Royal University, 80.7 per cent. took Honours
and Exhibitions ; of those from Blackrock College, 92.5
per cent. took Honours and Exhibitions. On the other
side, of those condidates from the Cork Queen’s College,
only 60.6 per cont. took Honours and Exhibitions; of
thoso from the Galway Queen’s College, 68.2 per cent.
took Honours and Exhibitions. Again, from the year -
1893 to 1900, in the medical examinations of the Royal .
Univorsity, the following shows the proportion of those
who took Exhibitions to those who passed the examina-

‘Whether Trinity College merits the traditional pres-
tige which is attached to it in Ireland as a seat of learn-
ing, we have no such data for determining as the Royal
University affords us in the case of the Queen’s Colleges.
T say, its prestige in Ireland, because outside Ireland it
has no prestige. Whilst the Queen’s Colleges stood out
in academical aloofness, a prestige was carefully manu-
factured for them, and they enjoyed it. It was easy;
for in those days the mere name of a secular or non-
Catholic College at once secured a prestige of academi-
cal superiority in Ireland. Then came the Royal Uni-
‘versity system which put that prestige to proof by
competition, and the prestige proved to be but a bubble.
‘When the bubble was blown off the prestige at once
collapsed, and although attempts to revive it have often
been made, it has never risen again.  That event makes
one reflect whether the prestige which Trinity College
still enjoys in Ireland may not be an air-bubble also. One
thing is, I believe, certain about that Institution; it
is the richest College in Europe. It owns 200,000 acres
of land confiscated in Munster and Ulster, besides other
considerable sources of income. Its total annual revenue
is variously estimated. = At any rate, is is enor-
mous. All its revenue is derived from Irish
sources, and mostly all from Catholic sources.
It has been enjoying its wealth since the 16th
century, and one may be excused for enquiring in the
20th century what has it done for Ireland?  What
value has it given for its wealth? TIts apologists are
fond of reminding Irish Catholics in. proof of its toler-
ant tendency always, that it opened its doors to them
so long ago as 1793. In any case, that would not be so
much the sign of a liberal spirit as of an awakening
sense of justice, since it was subsisting on Catholic re-
sources, and Catholics were the vast majority of the
nation. The great function of a University claiming
to be National is to leaven the nation as a whole, not a
favoured fraction of the people. But, setting that con-

that partial concession of right ; the privilege came from

tion to Father Dinneen’s latest book, if Mr. Flavin, say,
weroe to bring the matter before the Postmaster-General.
‘Litneata Gaevilge consists of an excellent chap-
ter on “Letter-writing in Irish,” with nine examples,
each consisting of a letter in Irish, and the reply, ex-
planatory notes, and a good list of place and personal
names. The author has written his book for the young,
but all students of Irish will find matter for study in
it. Even good Irish speakers are often ignorant of
how to begin and end a letter in Irish, using stilted
and often meaningless translations of English formulas,
instead of the more natural Irish phrases suggested by
Father Dinneen. Again our place names, and our per- .
sonal names, are so hopelessly corrupted in their Angli-
cized forms, that even advanced students would fail
to give many of them their proper Irish equivalents.
The lists which Father Dinneen gives in the appendix to
his book, solve their difficulty to a large extent.

Letter-writing in Irish is an invaluable exercise for
those who wish to acquire it as a living tongue—an
exerciso infinitely superior to translation from the Eng-
lish, and much easier than the writing of formal com-
positions or set themes. It is the exercise in writing
which comes nearest to the spoken use of the language.
For this reason it is to be recommended te students and
to teachers, and both classes will find in Licpeaca
Taevilge a necessary aid.

This is but a brief and unworthy notice of a small
but important work. I hope the book will soon be so
well known as to render any further notice unnecessary.
